title: How do I format citations correctly?
error_description: Unsure about citation format, inconsistent references
solution: Citations should follow your publication style guide (APA, MLA, Chicago, Harvard, or IEEE). Ensure consistency: (1) use the correct format for in-text citations, (2) maintain alphabetical ordering in reference lists, (3) verify DOI and URL formatting, (4) double-check author names and publication dates. Most publishing platforms provide citation templates.
aliases: citations, references, bibliography, citations format, citation style

title: What is the correct APA citation format?
error_description: APA citations formatted incorrectly
solution: APA format rules: (1) Author Last Name, First Initial. (Year). Title. Publisher. (2) In-text citations: (Author, Year) or Author (Year). (3) For direct quotes include page number: (Author, Year, p. XX). (4) Multiple authors: (Author1 & Author2, Year) for two authors, or (Author1 et al., Year) for three or more. (5) Arrange references alphabetically. (6) Use hanging indent for references. (7) Capitalize only first word and proper nouns in titles. Verify against latest APA style guide edition.
aliases: APA, APA citation, APA format, citation style, references

title: How do I add DOI and URL to citations?
error_description: DOI or URL formatting incorrect in citations
solution: DOI and URL guidelines: (1) DOI (Digital Object Identifier) format: https://doi.org/10.XXXX/XXXXX or doi: 10.XXXX/XXXXX. (2) URLs should be active and include http:// or https://. (3) In APA format: Author (Year). Title. Retrieved from https://url or doi: 10.XXXX. (4) For DOI-based citations, prefer DOI over URL when available. (5) Test URLs to ensure they are current and not broken. (6) Include access date for unstable web sources. (7) Keep DOI format consistent throughout document.
aliases: DOI, URL, digital object identifier, citations, references

title: What is proper in-text citation format?
error_description: In-text citations incorrect or inconsistent
solution: In-text citation rules: (1) Parenthetical style: (Author, Year) at end of sentence. (2) Narrative style: "Author (Year) states that..." in text. (3) Direct quotes: include page number (Author, Year, p. 123). (4) Multiple works by same author in same year: use a, b, c (Smith, 2020a), (Smith, 2020b). (5) Secondary sources: cite original author but note "as cited in". (6) For webpages without author: use title and year. (7) Ensure every citation appears in reference list. (8) Be consistent with citation style throughout.
aliases: in-text citations, citation placement, quotations, references

title: How do I manage multiple citations from same source?
error_description: Repeated citations from same source confusing
solution: Multiple citation rules: (1) First citation: full format with page number. (2) Subsequent citations: use shorthand form (Author, Year, p. XX). (3) For ibid. style: avoid in modern formats. (4) Use "op. cit." sparingly if required by style. (5) Consolidate multiple citations: (Author1, Year1; Author2, Year2). (6) Multiple pages from same source: use pp. XX-XX instead of p. for multiple. (7) Organize chronologically if citing multiple dates from same author. (8) Use note number system if using footnotes/endnotes for full citations.
aliases: repeated citations, citation shorthand, ibid, multiple sources

title: What are the most common citation style mistakes?
error_description: Citation formatting errors, inconsistent styles
solution: Common mistakes to avoid: (1) Mixing citation styles (don't combine APA with Chicago). (2) Missing page numbers in direct quotes. (3) Alphabetical ordering errors in reference list. (4) Incorrect capitalization (only first word and proper nouns). (5) Missing DOI or full URL. (6) Author names in wrong order. (7) Incorrect year format (should be in parentheses). (8) Missing publication information. (9) Inconsistent spacing or punctuation. (10) Not updating citations in final version. Use citation management tools (Zotero, Mendeley) to avoid errors.
aliases: citation errors, citation mistakes, formatting, consistency

title: What are common proofreading errors?
error_description: Need to identify and fix proofreading issues
solution: Common proofreading errors include: (1) spelling and grammar mistakes, (2) inconsistent capitalization, (3) misplaced punctuation, (4) repeated words, (5) tense inconsistencies, (6) formatting irregularities. Best practices: (1) read aloud to catch missed words, (2) use spell-check tools, (3) check headers and subheadings, (4) verify numbers and statistics, (5) proofread multiple times, (6) have someone else review for fresh perspective.
aliases: proofreading, editing, errors, grammar, spelling, typos

title: How do I proofread effectively?
error_description: Document has errors despite proofreading
solution: Effective proofreading strategies: (1) Proofread multiple times (different passes for different issues). (2) Change font or size to see text fresh. (3) Read backwards to check spelling. (4) Use spell-checker but manually verify homophones (their/there, to/too). (5) Check one element at a time (first pass: spelling, second: grammar, third: formatting). (6) Print hard copy if possible (different than screen). (7) Use text-to-speech to hear mistakes. (8) Allow time between writing and proofreading. (9) Have someone else review. (10) Keep a personal error checklist. (11) Be extra careful with proper names and numbers.
aliases: proofreading, quality control, review, editing, verification

title: What punctuation rules should I follow?
error_description: Inconsistent or incorrect punctuation
solution: Key punctuation rules: (1) Periods and commas inside quotes: "Like this." (2) Colons after complete clause when introducing list. (3) Semicolons to connect independent clauses. (4) Dashes (em-dash "—") set off phrases, not spaces around. (5) Ellipsis "..." shows omission (not "...."). (6) Apostrophes for contractions and possessives (not "it's" for "its"). (7) Consistent use of Oxford comma in lists. (8) Question marks outside quotes if question is outside quote. (9) Hyphenate compound modifiers before noun: "well-known author". (10) Parenthetical rules: period inside if complete sentence, outside if phrase.
aliases: punctuation, grammar, syntax, commas, periods, dashes

title: How do I fix grammar and tense issues?
error_description: Grammar errors, tense inconsistency
solution: Grammar and tense guidelines: (1) Maintain consistent tense (past for narrative, present for analysis). (2) Use parallel structure: "The plan includes reviewing, analyzing, and implementing." (3) Subject-verb agreement: "The data shows..." not "The data show...". (4) Pronoun clarity: clear antecedents for "this" and "that". (5) Avoid sentence fragments and run-ons. (6) Use active voice when possible: "The author wrote" vs "It was written". (7) Avoid double negatives. (8) Check modifier placement: "Only the results changed" vs "The results only changed". (9) Use correct verb forms (lay/lie, affect/effect). (10) Be consistent with number agreement throughout.
aliases: grammar, tense, verb, subject agreement, sentence structure

title: How do I ensure stylistic consistency?
error_description: Inconsistent style, formatting, or terminology
solution: Style consistency checklist: (1) Create a style guide document for your project. (2) Maintain consistent terminology (decide: "internet" or "Internet", "email" or "e-mail"). (3) Use consistent capitalization for key terms. (4) Define acronyms on first use: "ISSN (International Standard Serial Number)". (5) Consistent number formatting (spell out 0-9, numerals 10+, or follow style guide). (6) Consistent date format throughout. (7) Use consistent heading styles and capitalization. (8) Maintain consistent spacing and indentation. (9) Use style guide tools: brand guidelines, editorial guides, or Chicago Manual of Style. (10) Have consistency checking as separate proofreading pass.
aliases: style guide, consistency, terminology, formatting consistency

title: How do I handle hyphenation and word breaks?
error_description: Words hyphenated incorrectly or breaking in wrong places
solution: Hyphenation rules: (1) Hyphenate compound modifiers before noun: "well-known author", "first-time reader". (2) Don't hyphenate adverbs ending in -ly: "newly formed committee". (3) Don't hyphenate after prefixes unless needed for clarity: "re-cover" (cover again) vs "recover" (regain). (4) Use en-dash for ranges: "pages 45–67", "2020–2021". (5) Use em-dash (—) for breaks in thought. (6) In justified text, avoid excessive hyphenation—adjust spacing if needed. (7) Never separate single letters: don't end line with "a-" or start with "-pple". (8) Check style guide for specific words. (9) Be consistent with hyphenation choices throughout.
aliases: hyphenation, word breaks, dashes, compound words

title: How do I validate XML structure?
error_description: XML validation errors, DTD or schema issues
solution: To validate XML: (1) check well-formedness (all tags properly closed, correct nesting), (2) validate against DTD (Document Type Definition) or XSD (XML Schema Definition), (3) use online validators or IDE plugins, (4) check namespace declarations are correct, (5) verify all required elements are present, (6) ensure attribute values are quoted, (7) validate against the appropriate schema version for your publication format. Common issues: unclosed tags, incorrect nesting, missing namespace declarations.
aliases: XML validation, DTD, XSD, schema, well-formed XML, namespace

title: What is JATS XML and when should I use it?
error_description: Unclear about JATS format and requirements
solution: JATS (Journal Article Tag Suite) is the standard XML format for academic and journal articles. Use JATS when: (1) publishing in academic journals, (2) submitting to PubMed Central or similar repositories, (3) need cross-publisher compatibility. JATS structure includes: (1) article (root), (2) front (metadata: title, authors, abstract), (3) body (sections and content), (4) back (references, appendices). Key JATS elements: article-meta, contrib-group, element-citation, kwd-group, fig, table-wrap. JATS supports rich tagging for: affiliations, funding, permissions, custom metadata. Always validate against JATS version used (1.0, 1.1, 1.2, etc.).
aliases: JATS, JATS XML, journal article, XML standard, academic publishing

title: What are common JATS element errors?
error_description: JATS elements missing or incorrectly tagged
solution: Common JATS element issues: (1) Missing required article-meta section. (2) author/contrib not properly structured with contrib-group. (3) Incorrect element-citation format for references. (4) Missing named-content for keywords or custom metadata. (5) Improper nesting of contrib information (name, role, aff). (6) Missing pub-date or incorrect format. (7) Permissions element missing or incomplete. (8) Incorrect use of xlink attributes for cross-references. (9) Table structure using wrong JATS elements (table-wrap vs tbl). (10) Missing or incorrect namespace declaration. Check JATS documentation for version you're using.
aliases: JATS errors, element structure, JATS elements, validation

title: How do I handle special characters in XML?
error_description: Special characters not rendering correctly in XML
solution: XML special character handling: (1) Use character entities: < (<), > (>), & (&), " ("), ' (') (2) For non-ASCII characters: use numeric entities   or use UTF-8 encoding. (3) CDATA sections for literal text: <![CDATA[<html>code</html>]]> (4) Declare encoding in XML declaration: <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> (5) Avoid using special characters in element names or attributes. (6) For math symbols: use Unicode entities or MathML elements. (7) Test character display in multiple output formats. (8) Document any custom character mappings. (9) Be consistent with entity usage throughout.
aliases: XML entities, special characters, encoding, unicode, CDATA

title: What is the correct author attribution structure in JATS?
error_description: Author information not properly structured in JATS
solution: JATS author structure: (1) Use contrib-group within front/article-meta. (2) Each author is a contrib element with contrib-type="author". (3) Inside contrib: name element (surname, given-names), xref to affiliations, roles if needed. (4) Affiliations: use aff element with id, connect via xref. (5) Corresponding author: mark with corresp element or xref[@ref-type="corresp"]. (6) Author order matters—list in document order. (7) Example: <contrib-group><contrib contrib-type="author"><name><surname>Smith</surname><given-names>John</given-names></name><xref ref-type="aff" rid="aff1"/></contrib><aff id="aff1"><institution>University</institution></aff></contrib-group> (8) Include ORCID if available using contrib-id.
aliases: JATS authors, author attribution, contrib-group, affiliations

title: How do I properly tag references in JATS?
error_description: References not properly tagged in JATS format
solution: JATS reference tagging: (1) All references in back/ref-list element. (2) Each reference is a ref element with optional id attribute. (3) Reference content in element-citation: person-group (authors), article-title, source (journal title), year, volume, fpage/lpage (page range). (4) Person group structure: name or collab elements, with person-group-type="author/editor". (5) For journal articles: include DOI in pub-id element. (6) For books: include publisher element. (7) Consecutive numbering or use citation keys. (8) Validate reference structure against JATS DTD. Example: <ref id="ref1"><element-citation publication-type="journal"><person-group><name><surname>Smith</surname></name></person-group><article-title>Title</article-title><source>Journal</source><year>2020</year></element-citation></ref>
aliases: JATS references, element-citation, reference list, citations

title: What does well-formed XML mean?
error_description: XML parsing errors, not well-formed
solution: Well-formed XML requirements: (1) Single root element wrapping all content. (2) All elements properly closed (<tag>content</tag>). (3) Correct nesting—no overlapping tags. (4) Attribute values must be quoted. (5) Special characters properly escaped (<, >, &, ", '). (6) XML declaration optional but recommended: <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> (7) Case-sensitive: <Tag> ≠ <tag>. (8) No spaces in element names. (9) Cannot start element name with number or punctuation. (10) To check: parse with XML parser or validator. Most errors are simple: missing closing tag, unquoted attribute, or wrong character encoding.
aliases: well-formed XML, XML parsing, XML errors, syntax

title: How do I use namespaces in XML?
error_description: Namespace errors or confusion in XML documents
solution: XML namespaces: (1) Prevent element name conflicts by prefixing. (2) Declare with xmlns attribute: xmlns:prefix="http://example.com/ns". (3) Default namespace (no prefix): xmlns="http://example.com/ns". (4) Use in elements: <prefix:element> (5) URLs don't need to resolve—they're unique identifiers. (6) Common namespaces: xlink (linking), xml (reserved), custom namespaces. (7) Scope: namespace declaration applies to element and children. (8) Multiple namespaces in one document: each needs own prefix. (9) Schema validation: namespaces must match schema declarations. (10) Example: <article xmlns="http://jats.nlm.nih.gov"> declares JATS namespace for all children. Check JATS or your format's namespace documentation.
aliases: namespaces, xmlns, namespace declaration, XML schema

title: What is DTD and why is it important?
error_description: DTD validation errors or unclear what DTD does
solution: DTD (Document Type Definition) specifies rules for XML structure: (1) Which elements are allowed, (2) Which attributes each element can have, (3) Required vs optional elements, (4) Element nesting rules, (5) Cardinality (how many times element appears). Importance: (1) Ensures consistency across documents, (2) Catches structural errors early, (3) Required for valid XML, (4) Enables cross-publisher compatibility. DTD vs XSD: DTD is simpler, older; XSD (Schema) is newer, more powerful. Both serve same validation purpose. Most publishing formats require DTD validation before distribution.
aliases: DTD, document type definition, validation, DTD errors

title: How do I fix common DTD validation errors?
error_description: DTD validation failing with errors
solution: Common DTD errors and fixes: (1) "Element X not declared" → add element to DTD or remove from document. (2) "Missing required element Y" → add missing element. (3) "Unexpected element Z" → element not allowed in current context (check nesting). (4) "Duplicate attribute" → attribute appears twice (remove duplicate). (5) "Invalid character in attribute" → quote attribute value properly. (6) "Element order incorrect" → elements must follow DTD sequence (fix order). (7) "ID not unique" → duplicate id attribute (use unique IDs). (8) To debug: use XML validator tool with DTD file, read error messages carefully noting element and line number, check DTD rules for that element, compare against DTD documentation.
aliases: DTD errors, validation errors, element declaration, nesting

title: How do I validate against JATS DTD?
error_description: Unable to validate XML against JATS DTD
solution: JATS DTD validation steps: (1) Download JATS DTD files from NLM (National Library of Medicine). (2) Ensure XML declares DOCTYPE: <!DOCTYPE article SYSTEM "jats.dtd"> (3) Place DTD file in accessible location relative to XML. (4) Use validator: XMLMind, Oxygen XML Editor, or online validators. (5) Check JATS version matches your XML (1.0, 1.1, 1.2, etc.). (6) Common issues: incorrect DOCTYPE declaration, DTD file path wrong, missing supporting .ent files. (7) Ensure XML is well-formed first (not a DTD issue). (8) Read error messages carefully—they point to exact line/element. (9) Reference: https://jats.nlm.nih.gov/ for JATS documentation and DTD files.
aliases: JATS DTD, validation, NLM, DOCTYPE, DTD files

title: What is the difference between DTD and XSD?
error_description: Confused about DTD vs XSD
solution: DTD vs XSD comparison: DTD (Document Type Definition): Older standard, simpler syntax, limited type checking, used in JATS. XSD (XML Schema Definition): Newer standard, more powerful, supports data types, better validation. When to use: (1) DTD if required by standard (JATS, BITS, etc.), (2) XSD if creating new schema or need advanced features. Advantages DTD: simple, lightweight, legacy compatibility. Advantages XSD: stronger typing, namespace support, reusable components, clearer error messages. Many systems support both. For academic publishing, JATS uses DTD; others may use XSD. Choose based on your publishing standard and tooling requirements.
aliases: DTD, XSD, schema, validation, XML schema definition

title: How do I understand DTD error messages?
error_description: DTD error messages unclear
solution: Decoding DTD error messages: (1) "Element X: unexpected element Y" → Y cannot appear inside X (check DTD rules). (2) "Content model error" → element children not in correct order. (3) "Missing required element" → element declared as REQUIRED is not present. (4) "Invalid attribute value" → attribute value doesn't match defined type. (5) Error location: note line and column numbers. (6) Common causes: forgotten closing tag (affects all following), element name typo, wrong nesting. (7) Fix strategy: (a) identify offending element, (b) check DTD specification, (c) read element's allowed content, (d) compare against document. (8) Use visual tools to see element tree structure. (9) Validate incrementally—fix errors one at a time from top of file down.
aliases: error messages, DTD errors, validation errors, debugging

title: What are standard table formatting guidelines?
error_description: Tables not formatted consistently, alignment issues
solution: Standard table formatting: (1) use consistent column widths and alignment, (2) include descriptive headers in the first row, (3) alternate row colors for readability (optional but helpful), (4) use clear borders or spacing, (5) keep table captions above or below consistently, (6) ensure tables fit page width, (7) avoid splitting tables across pages if possible, (8) use consistent decimal alignment for numbers, (9) provide a descriptive table title and numbering sequence.
aliases: tables, table format, table design, formatting tables, table layout

title: How do I properly structure tables in XML?
error_description: Table XML structure incorrect or invalid
solution:
XML table structure (JATS table-wrap): (1) Wrap table in table-wrap element with optional id. (2) Include label (table number): (3) Include title: Table Description (4) Table element contains: thead (headers) and tbody (body rows). (5) Each row is tr element. (6) Header cells: th with @scope="col" or "row". (7) Data cells: td elements. (8) Attributes: @align, @valign for alignment, @colspan, @rowspan. (9) Notes after table: footnote text (10) Example: Results
Column 1
Data
aliases: table structure, XML tables, JATS tables, table formatting

title: How do I fix table layout and alignment problems?
error_description: Tables misaligned, overflowing, or not rendering properly
solution: Table layout fixes: (1) Check column widths don't exceed page width. (2) For PDF: use fixed layout, specify column widths explicitly. (3) For ePub/HTML: use flexible widths with min-width constraints. (4) Alignment: use text-align (left/center/right) and vertical-align (top/middle/bottom). (5) For numbers: right-align with consistent decimal position. (6) Avoid excessive spanning (colspan/rowspan makes tables complex). (7) Split large tables into multiple smaller tables if needed. (8) Use consistent cell padding and spacing. (9) Test table rendering in all output formats. (10) Use CSS for styling rather than inline formatting. (11) Ensure readability: adequate contrast, sufficient spacing between cells.
aliases: table alignment, table layout, table formatting, overflow

title: How do I format numbers and text in tables?
error_description: Numbers misaligned, text formatting inconsistent in tables
solution: Table content formatting: (1) Numbers: right-align and align decimals vertically. (2) Currency: include symbol ($ ¥ €) and align right. (3) Percentages: use % symbol consistently, align right. (4) Headers: bold, center, or left-align depending on content. (5) Negative numbers: use minus sign (−) or parentheses consistently. (6) Units: include consistently (kg, %, etc.). (7) Significant figures: match throughout table. (8) Missing data: use consistent notation (n/a, —, ?) (9) Text in cells: left-align, wrap if needed. (10) Avoid merged cells when possible (accessibility issue). (11) Use consistent font and sizing throughout table. (12) Use abbreviations sparingly; explain in footnote if needed.
aliases: number formatting, cell formatting, table content, alignment

title: How do I create accessible tables?
error_description: Tables not accessible to screen readers
solution: Accessible table guidelines: (1) Use semantic <th> for headers with @scope="col" or "row". (2) Provide table <caption> or aria-label. (3) Avoid merged cells (thead/tbody merge rows). (4) Use consistent column/row structure. (5) Header cells must precede data cells. (6) For complex tables: provide summary. (7) Use table headers markup: @headers attribute linking cells to headers. (8) Ensure sufficient color contrast. (9) Don't rely on color alone to distinguish cells. (10) Include row/column identifiers if needed. (11) Test with screen readers. (12) In JATS: use thead/tbody structure, th elements with @scope. Following these practices helps visually impaired users and search engines understand table content.
aliases: table accessibility, WCAG, screen reader, semantic tables

title: What are common publishing workflow errors?
error_description: Encountering errors in publishing process
solution: Common publishing errors: (1) Encoding errors (UTF-8 vs other encodings). (2) Missing images or broken image links. (3) Invalid metadata or incomplete fields. (4) Formatting lost during conversion. (5) Broken cross-references or hyperlinks. (6) Font issues in PDF. (7) Color profile mismatches. (8) Inconsistent styling between formats. (9) Missing page numbers or headers/footers. (10) Version control issues (outdated file). (11) Conflicting software versions. (12) Insufficient disk space. To prevent: establish quality control process, test all stages, use version control, maintain checklists, document procedures.
aliases: errors, troubleshooting, publishing issues, problems

title: How do I fix encoding and character issues?
error_description: Characters displaying incorrectly, encoding errors
solution: Encoding troubleshooting: (1) Check file encoding: open in text editor, look for BOM or encoding declaration. (2) Convert to UTF-8: most compatible encoding. (3) In XML: declare encoding in prolog: <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> (4) In HTML: use <meta charset="UTF-8">. (5) Check text editor settings: save as UTF-8. (6) Look for encoding declaration mismatch (file saved as UTF-8 but declared as Latin-1). (7) Replace smart quotes/dashes with standard versions. (8) Use character entity references for special chars. (9) Test in multiple software (Word, browsers, PDF viewers). (10) If conversion needed: use iconv or online converters. (11) Avoid mixing encoding within single document.
aliases: encoding, character issues, UTF-8, text encoding, special characters

title: How do I handle missing or broken images?
error_description: Images not displaying, broken image links
solution: Image troubleshooting: (1) Check file paths: use relative paths or verify absolute paths. (2) Ensure image files exist in correct location. (3) Use correct file format for output (PNG, JPEG, GIF, SVG). (4) Check image filename for typos or case-sensitivity issues. (5) Verify image permissions (readable file). (6) Check image dimensions and DPI. (7) In XML: use correct xlink:href syntax: <graphic xlink:href="image.jpg"/>. (8) In HTML: verify src attribute path. (9) Test in different software to isolate problem. (10) Check for file corruption: try re-saving image. (11) Ensure image encoding matches document. (12) Provide alt text for accessibility.
aliases: images, broken links, image errors, missing images, file paths

title: How do I debug conversion errors?
error_description: Document conversion failing or producing errors
solution: Conversion debugging steps: (1) Check source file is valid (well-formed XML, correct format). (2) Verify conversion tool supports your file format. (3) Look at conversion logs for specific error messages. (4) Test with simpler document first (narrow down cause). (5) Check for unsupported features (complex formatting, scripts). (6) Verify file encoding is correct. (7) Try different output format to isolate problem. (8) Update conversion tool to latest version. (9) Check available disk space and memory. (10) Validate output against target schema. (11) Compare input vs output carefully. (12) Contact tool support with error logs and sample file.
aliases: conversion errors, troubleshooting, file conversion, debugging

title: How do I handle version control and conflicts?
error_description: Version conflicts, lost changes, confused versions
solution: Version control best practices: (1) Use version control system (Git, SVN). (2) Use meaningful commit messages. (3) Tag release versions: v1.0, v1.1, v2.0. (4) Create branches for major changes. (5) Use descriptive filenames with versions: manuscript_v1.docx, not "final_final_FINAL". (6) Keep backup copies in separate location. (7) Document version history/changelog. (8) Use naming convention: version numbers (1.0, 1.1) or dates (2021-03, 2021-04). (9) When merging versions, clearly mark conflicts. (10) Only keep latest version in main location. (11) Archive old versions. (12) Communicate version updates to all collaborators.
aliases: version control, Git, versioning, conflicts, changelog

title: How do I convert to EPUB format?
error_description: Conversion to ePub failing or producing errors
solution: EPUB conversion best practices: (1) structure content with proper HTML semantics, (2) use CSS for styling (limited but essential), (3) embed images with proper paths and compression, (4) test in multiple readers (Apple Books, Kindle, Adobe Digital Editions), (5) avoid absolute positioning and fixed layouts, (6) include cover image and metadata, (7) validate EPUB file against schema, (8) use tools like Calibre or Sigil for conversion and testing, (9) ensure fonts are licensed for embedding.
aliases: ePub, ebook conversion, ePub format, epub file, digital publishing

title: How do I fix EPUB rendering issues?
error_description: EPUB displays incorrectly in readers
solution: EPUB rendering fixes: (1) Validate EPUB against schema (use EpubCheck). (2) Check for common issues: absolute positioning, fixed widths, complex CSS. (3) Test in multiple readers (different behavior). (4) Simplify CSS: use basic properties, avoid advanced features. (5) Ensure images are embedded and paths correct. (6) Check font licensing and embedding. (7) Use relative units (em, %) not absolute (px). (8) Ensure proper XHTML structure. (9) Check metadata completeness. (10) Look for file encoding issues. (11) Rebuild EPUB file if corrupted. (12) Use Sigil to inspect and debug EPUB structure.
aliases: EPUB errors, rendering, display issues, ebook format

title: What is the difference between PDF and EPUB?
error_description: Unsure which format to use
solution: PDF vs ePub: PDF is fixed-layout (for print-like appearance, better for precise formatting), ePub is reflowable (adapts to device screen size, better for e-readers). Use PDF for: books requiring precise layout, documents with complex formatting, print-ready files. Use ePub for: e-readers, flexible text sizing, mobile reading. Hybrid approach: create both formats for maximum reach. Consider your distribution channel and reader device when choosing.
aliases: PDF, ePub, format comparison, PDF vs ePub, file format, ebook format

title: How do I ensure PDF quality and compatibility?
error_description: PDF quality issues or incompatibility problems
solution: PDF quality guidelines: (1) Use PDF/A format for long-term archival. (2) Embed all fonts for compatibility. (3) Use appropriate color profile (sRGB for screen, CMYK for print). (4) Set PDF version (1.4 is widely compatible). (5) Include metadata (title, author, keywords). (6) Use proper compression without losing quality. (7) Test on multiple viewers and devices. (8) Ensure hyperlinks work. (9) Include bookmarks for navigation. (10) Add security/permissions if needed. (11) Verify image resolution (300 DPI for print, 72 DPI for screen). (12) Check for accessibility (tagged PDF).
aliases: PDF quality, compatibility, PDF/A, archival, PDF formatting

title: How do I fix PDF rendering or display issues?
error_description: PDF displays incorrectly or corrupted
solution: PDF troubleshooting: (1) Verify PDF is well-formed: try opening in multiple viewers. (2) Regenerate PDF from source (may fix corruption). (3) Check page orientation (portrait/landscape). (4) Look for font issues: missing or embedding problems. (5) Check image embedding and compression. (6) Test in different PDF viewers (Adobe, Preview, browser). (7) Try updating PDF viewer software. (8) Check file size—very large PDFs may have compression issues. (9) Repair corrupted PDF: use online tools or PDF software. (10) Verify PDF version compatibility with viewer. (11) Check for security restrictions (password, permissions). (12) Examine PDF structure using PDF editor.
aliases: PDF errors, display issues, PDF corruption, rendering

title: How do I handle PDF color management?
error_description: PDF colors look wrong in print or screen
solution: PDF color management: (1) Use sRGB for screen-only PDFs. (2) Use CMYK for print PDFs. (3) Include color profile (embedded ICC profile). (4) Avoid RGB in print PDFs (will be converted). (5) Test color appearance on multiple devices. (6) Use Pantone colors for spot color printing. (7) Set default colors in PDF settings. (8) Document color settings in metadata. (9) Test with professional color tools if critical. (10) Ensure printer supports chosen color profile. (11) Communicate color expectations to printer. (12) Check for color conversion settings in PDF creation software.
aliases: PDF colors, color profile, CMYK, RGB, sRGB, print colors

title: What is Schematron validation?
error_description: Unsure about Schematron or how to use it
solution: Schematron is an XML validation language that: (1) Uses XPath to define rules/patterns. (2) More flexible than DTD/XSD for complex rules. (3) Can enforce business logic not possible in DTD. (4) Provides meaningful error messages. (5) Common in publishing: JATS, BITS use Schematron rules. (6) Rules: <pattern> contains <rule> which contains <assert> conditions. (7) Example: assert that every figure has a caption. (8) Can reference external rules. (9) Validates after XML is well-formed and schema-valid. (10) Useful for: domain-specific rules, cross-element validation, context-dependent rules. (11) Tools: oXygen XML, Schematron tools. (12) Reference: ISO/IEC 19757-3 or Schematron.com
aliases: Schematron, validation, rules, XPath, Schematron rules

title: How do I validate EPUB files?
error_description: EPUB validation errors or failing validation
solution: EPUB validation: (1) Use EpubCheck (official tool from W3C/IDPF). (2) Run command: epubcheck filename.epub (3) Check output for errors and warnings. (4) Common issues: missing metadata, invalid XHTML, broken links, file structure errors. (5) Fix errors before fixing warnings. (6) Validate content.opf file (manifest, spine, metadata). (7) Validate XHTML files individually if needed. (8) Check mimetype file is first (uncompressed). (9) Verify file permissions and paths. (10) Use online validators if offline tools unavailable. (11) Repeat validation after each fix. (12) Reference: EpubCheck documentation and EPUB3 spec.
aliases: EPUB validation, EpubCheck, errors, validation tools

title: What is PMC and when is it required?
error_description: Unclear about PMC submission requirements
solution: PMC (PubMed Central) is the free full-text biomedical journal archive. Requirements: (1) Requires JATS XML format. (2) Must include complete metadata. (3) Must validate against PMC JATS DTD. (4) Journal-specific requirements vary. (5) Permissions must be properly tagged. (6) All figures and tables required. (7) References must be complete. (8) Use PMC sample files for format examples. (9) Submit via NIH manuscript management system. (10) Validation occurs during submission. (11) Compliance needed for NIH funding acknowledgment. (12) Reference: PMC documentation at ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc
aliases: PMC, PubMed Central, JATS, submission, biomedical journals

title: What is Crossref and DOI registration?
error_description: Unsure about Crossref or DOI registration
solution: Crossref DOI registration: (1) Crossref registers DOIs and metadata. (2) Required for most academic/journal articles. (3) DOI format: https://doi.org/10.XXXX/XXXXX (4) Register metadata including: title, authors, publication date, volume, issue, pages. (5) Use XML submission (JATS XML preferred). (6) Metadata must be complete and accurate. (7) Registration fee applies (publisher pays). (8) DOIs persist indefinitely. (9) Enables citation tracking and discovery. (10) JATS XML with DOI: use pub-id element with @pub-id-type="doi". (11) Required for: most journals, conferences (if published). (12) Reference: crossref.org for registration and documentation.
aliases: Crossref, DOI, digital object identifier, registration, metadata

title: What is ISSN and ISBN?
error_description: Confused about ISSN vs ISBN
solution: ISSN vs ISBN: ISSN (International Standard Serial Number): 8-digit code for journals, magazines, continuing publications, format: XXXX-XXXX. ISBN (International Standard Book Number): 13-digit code for books, format: 978-X-XXXX-XXXX-X. When to use: (1) ISSN: any serial publication (journal, magazine, series). (2) ISBN: one-off books or monographs. (3) Both: some publications have both (book series). (4) Registration: ISSN through national agencies, ISBN through Bowker or national agencies. (5) Include in metadata for discoverability. (6) Required for: most academic publications, commercial books. (7) Free to register ISSN, ISBN costs vary. (8) Check: issn.org for ISSN, isbn-international.org for ISBN.
aliases: ISSN, ISBN, identifier, journal, book, metadata

title: How do I ensure WCAG accessibility compliance?
error_description: Document not meeting WCAG accessibility standards
solution: WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) compliance: (1) Level A: minimum compliance (avoid, aim higher). (2) Level AA: recommended, covers most users. (3) Level AAA: enhanced compliance. (4) Four principles: Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust. (5) Key requirements: color contrast (4.5:1), alt text, heading hierarchy, keyboard navigation. (6) Check: (a) Color contrast ratio (use contrast checker tool), (b) Text alternatives for images, (c) Proper HTML structure, (d) Form labels. (7) Test with screen readers. (8) Use WAVE or axe accessibility tools. (9) Include captions/transcripts for audio/video. (10) Avoid PDF if possible (accessibility difficult). (11) Make tagged PDFs if PDF required. (12) Reference: WCAG 2.1 guidelines at w3.org
aliases: WCAG, accessibility, compliance, A11y, accessible

title: What is document tagging and why is it important?
error_description: Unsure about document tagging
solution: Document tagging: (1) Adding metadata/semantic structure to document. (2) Essential for accessibility and searchability. (3) Types: (a) XML/semantic tags, (b) PDF tags, (c) metadata tags. (4) Benefits: (a) Screen readers understand content, (b) Improved SEO, (c) Better document management, (d) Enables advanced features. (5) PDF tagging: read order, alt text for images, form fields. (6) XML tagging: semantic meaning (heading, paragraph, figure). (7) Tools: Acrobat (PDF), oXygen (XML), publishing platforms. (8) Best practice: tag during creation, not after. (9) Standards: PDF/UA for accessible PDFs, WCAG for web. (10) Validation: test with screen readers. (11) Time investment: more upfront, but saves time in long run.
aliases: tagging, semantic tagging, PDF tagging, metadata, structure

title: How do I fix font embedding issues?
error_description: Fonts not embedding correctly in PDF
solution: Font embedding: (1) verify all fonts are licensed for embedding, (2) in PDF creation settings, ensure "Embed all fonts" is selected, (3) use standard fonts (Arial, Times New Roman, Helvetica) as fallback, (4) avoid custom fonts without embedding rights, (5) test PDF on different devices to verify font display, (6) subset fonts to reduce file size if supported, (7) avoid very light or decorative fonts for body text, (8) check that all font variations (bold, italic) are embedded, (9) document font choices for consistency.
aliases: fonts, font embedding, PDF fonts, font issues, typeface

title: How do I ensure color consistency?
error_description: Colors look different in print vs. screen
solution: Color consistency: (1) use color profiles (sRGB for screen, CMYK for print), (2) test colors on multiple devices and printers, (3) avoid relying on color alone for important information (add patterns/labels), (4) use named colors for consistency across files, (5) check color accessibility for colorblind readers, (6) maintain color palette document, (7) note that screen colors are RGB and print colors are CMYK, (8) expect slight variations between devices, (9) use professional calibration tools for critical color work, (10) specify Pantone colors for spot color printing if needed.
aliases: color, colors, RGB, CMYK, color profile, color consistency

title: What fonts are best for publishing?
error_description: Unsure which fonts to use for publication
solution: Font selection guidelines: (1) Serif fonts (Times New Roman, Garamond) for body text—easier to read in print. (2) Sans-serif (Arial, Helvetica) for headings and screen. (3) Avoid decorative fonts for body text—hard to read. (4) Use consistent font pairing (typically serif + sans-serif). (5) Ensure sufficient font size (minimum 10pt for body, larger for headings). (6) Standard fonts (widely available): Arial, Times New Roman, Helvetica, Courier. (7) Web-safe fonts for digital: Georgia, Verdana, Tahoma. (8) Licensing: check embedding rights for PDF. (9) Accessibility: avoid very light weights, ensure good contrast. (10) Consistency: stick to 2-3 font families maximum. (11) Test: different sizes, weights, and devices.
aliases: fonts, font selection, typography, typeface, font pairing

title: How do I handle international characters and fonts?
error_description: International characters not displaying, font issues with non-Latin scripts
solution: International character handling: (1) Use UTF-8 encoding (supports all languages). (2) Select fonts supporting required scripts (Arial Unicode, Noto fonts). (3) For CJK (Chinese, Japanese, Korean): use CJK-specific fonts. (4) For Arabic/Hebrew: use fonts supporting right-to-left. (5) Verify font license includes all needed scripts. (6) Test rendering in target software. (7) For PDF: ensure fonts are embedded. (8) For web: use @font-face with fallbacks. (9) In XML: ensure encoding declared correctly. (10) Document character support. (11) Consider user device capabilities. (12) Use language tags (lang attribute) in HTML/XML for proper rendering.
aliases: international characters, fonts, CJK, Unicode, right-to-left

title: What are best practices for image handling?
error_description: Images blurry, low resolution, or scaling incorrectly
solution: Image best practices: (1) use at least 300 DPI for print, 72 DPI for screen, (2) use RGB for web, CMYK for print, (3) compress images to reduce file size without losing quality, (4) use appropriate formats (PNG for graphics, JPEG for photos), (5) provide alt text for accessibility, (6) include proper image captions and numbering, (7) ensure consistent sizing across similar images, (8) place images near relevant text, (9) test how images render in final output format, (10) credit image sources if required.
aliases: images, image resolution, DPI, image quality, image compression, photos

title: How do I properly tag and caption figures in XML?
error_description: Figures not properly structured or captioned in XML
solution:
Figure tagging in JATS: (1) Wrap in fig element with optional id. (2) Include label: . (3) Include caption: Figure Title

Description

. (4) Include image: . (5) For multiple images: use subfig elements. (6) Permissions if needed: ©. (7) Add alt text: description for accessibility. (8) Note: provide context. (9) Number figures sequentially. (10) Cross-reference in text. (11) Example: Results
aliases: figures, images, captions, JATS, XML structure

title: How do I handle supplementary materials?
error_description: Unsure how to structure supplementary materials
solution: Supplementary materials guidelines: (1) Store separately from main document. (2) Use descriptive filenames: "supplement_table_1.xlsx" not "sup1.zip". (3) Include in metadata/supplement list. (4) Provide format, size, and description. (5) Ensure supplementary files are in accessible formats. (6) Link from main document. (7) In JATS: use supp-material element. (8) Number and title consistently. (9) Include with submission. (10) Test access before publication. (11) Archive permanently with main publication. (12) Consider creating index or TOC for multiple supplements.
aliases: supplementary materials, supplements, appendices, supporting files

title: What is the correct heading hierarchy?
error_description: Heading levels inconsistent or incorrect
solution: Proper heading hierarchy: (1) use H1 only once per page for the main title, (2) follow sequential order (H1 → H2 → H3, not H1 → H3), (3) H2 for major sections, H3 for subsections, H4 for sub-subsections, (4) skip levels only with justification, (5) avoid styling headings just for appearance (use CSS instead), (6) ensure headings accurately describe section content, (7) use consistent capitalization style throughout, (8) this improves both SEO and accessibility.
aliases: headings, heading levels, H1, H2, H3, heading structure, hierarchy

title: How do I structure document sections properly?
error_description: Document structure unclear or inconsistent
solution:
Section structure: (1) Use sec element for sections in JATS. (2) Include title: Section Name. (3) Maintain hierarchical nesting: sec > sec (nested for subsections). (4) Each section has unique meaningful title. (5) Logical grouping of related content. (6) Consistent naming (e.g., "Introduction", "Methods", "Results", "Discussion"). (7) Proper nesting depth (don't nest more than 3-4 levels). (8) Cross-references between sections. (9) Alternative: use article structure with sec elements directly. (10) Example: Introduction

Content

Background

Subsection content
aliases: section structure, document structure, hierarchy, JATS sections

title: How do I handle cross-references?
error_description: Cross-references broken or pointing to wrong sections
solution: Cross-reference management: (1) use unique, descriptive IDs for sections/figures/tables, (2) reference by ID, not by page number (improves flexibility), (3) update all references when moving content, (4) use "See Chapter 3" instead of "See page 45" for digital formats, (5) use in-document linking where supported, (6) test all cross-references before publishing, (7) maintain a reference index, (8) consider using tools with automatic cross-reference updating, (9) clearly label reference types (Chapter, Figure, Table, etc.).
aliases: cross-references, references, links, cross reference, internal links

title: What metadata should I include?
error_description: Missing or incomplete metadata
solution: Essential metadata: (1) Title and subtitle, (2) Author(s) name, (3) Publication date, (4) ISBN/ISSN if applicable, (5) Language code, (6) Description/abstract, (7) Keywords/tags, (8) Rights and licensing information, (9) Publisher information, (10) Table of contents, (11) Cover image, (12) Edition information, (13) Target audience. Metadata improves discoverability, SEO, and accessibility. Ensure consistency with your catalog system.
aliases: metadata, title, author, ISBN, publication date, information

title: How do I handle publication dates and versions?
error_description: Confusion about publication dates, versions
solution: Date and version guidelines: (1) Publication date: official release date. (2) Version date: when specific edition released. (3) Format: ISO 8601 standard (YYYY-MM-DD). (4) Include in metadata consistently. (5) Track publication history (first published vs. reprinted). (6) Include version number: 1.0, 1.1, 2.0. (7) Differentiate between draft and published. (8) Archive dates for revisions. (9) In JATS: pub-date element with @pub-type attribute. (10) For updates: include both original date and update date. (11) Communicate version changes. (12) Document version history in changelog.
aliases: publication date, version date, version history, dating documents

title: How do I handle keywords and tagging?
error_description: Unsure about keyword selection and tagging
solution: Keyword and tagging best practices: (1) 5-10 keywords per document (not too few, not too many). (2) Use controlled vocabulary if available (domain-specific thesaurus). (3) Include both specific and general terms. (4) Think about how users would search. (5) Prefer nouns and noun phrases over verbs. (6) Capitalize consistently. (7) Use synonyms sparingly (pick one standard form). (8) In JATS: kwd-group element with kwd sub-elements. (9) Language tagging: specify language of keywords. (10) Alphabetize if space-limited. (11) Review for relevance and accuracy. (12) Update if document is revised significantly.
aliases: keywords, tagging, search terms, metadata, kwd-group

title: How do I handle author and contributor information?
error_description: Unclear about author vs contributor roles
solution: Author and contributor roles: (1) Author: wrote content, takes responsibility. (2) Editor: reviewed and organized content. (3) Contributor: provided specific content (section, data, etc.). (4) Illustrator: created artwork/figures. (5) Translator: translated content. (6) Other roles as needed (reviewer, advisor, etc.). (7) In JATS: contrib element with @contrib-type attribute. (8) Include affiliations for each author. (9) Order matters: primary author first. (10) Include ORCID if available. (11) Use consistent name format. (12) Indicate corresponding author (for contact).
aliases: authors, contributors, roles, affiliations, ORCID, attribution

title: How do I handle licensing and permissions?
error_description: Confused about licensing and permissions
solution: Licensing and permissions: (1) Choose appropriate license (CC-BY, CC-BY-SA, CC-BY-NC, all rights reserved, etc.). (2) Include license statement and URL. (3) Creative Commons licenses: (a) CC-BY (most open), (b) CC-BY-SA (share alike), (c) CC-BY-NC (non-commercial), (d) CC0 (public domain). (4) Copyright notice: © [Year] [Owner]. (5) In JATS: permissions element. (6) In EPUB/PDF: include in copyright page. (7) Be clear about reuse rights. (8) Respect third-party content (get permissions, cite sources). (9) Include license logo if desired. (10) Document which parts have different licenses. (11) Update license only if new version released. (12) Reference: creativecommons.org
aliases: licensing, permissions, copyright, creative commons, CC-BY

title: How do I optimize for accessibility?
error_description: Document not accessible to all readers
solution: Accessibility best practices: (1) use semantic HTML/XML structure, (2) provide alt text for all images, (3) ensure sufficient color contrast (4.5:1 for normal text), (4) use descriptive link text (not "click here"), (5) provide transcripts for audio/video, (6) use proper heading hierarchy, (7) ensure content is keyboard navigable, (8) avoid flickering content, (9) test with screen reader software, (10) include table headers and captions, (11) make PDFs tagged for screen readers, (12) test with accessibility tools and real users.
aliases: accessibility, accessible, WCAG, screen reader, alt text, inclusive

title: How do I manage document versions?
error_description: Multiple versions causing confusion
solution: Version control: (1) use version numbers (1.0, 1.1, 2.0, etc.), (2) maintain a changelog documenting all changes, (3) keep dated backups, (4) use version control systems (Git) for text-based files, (5) clearly label draft vs. final, (6) include version history in metadata, (7) consider using collaborative tools (Google Docs, Overleaf), (8) establish naming conventions for file versions, (9) archive old versions separately, (10) communicate version changes to all stakeholders.
aliases: version control, versioning, document versions, version history, changelog
